mnemonics.title: RE Languages
mnemonics.content: RE = REject or loop forever for no-instances, but Accept for yes-instances. Think 'R' for Reject/Recurse (loop)

mnemonics.title: Decidable vs Undecidable
mnemonics.content: DFA: D- D- Easy (Decidable) | CFL: C- Can do (Decidable) | TM: T- Tough (Undecidable)

mnemonics.title: Rice's Theorem
mnemonics.content: Rice's Theorem: Any non-trivial property of TM languages is undecidable. Non-trivial = not always true, not always false.

mnemonics.title: Halting Problem Proof
mnemonics.content: Assume H decides Halting → Build D that loops when H says halts, halts when H says loops → Paradox when D runs on itself!

acronyms.usage: Class of languages for which a TM can accept all yes-instances (may loop on no)
Theoretical model that defines computability; any algorithm can be simulated by a TM
Undecidable problem used to prove other problems undecidable via reduction
Input: ⟨M, w⟩ - does M accept w? Undecidable, RE but not recursive
Input: ⟨M⟩ - is L(M) = ∅? Undecidable - cannot determine if TM accepts any string
Do two TMs recognize the same language? Undecidable

acronyms.expansion: All Strings Problem for Turing Machines
acronyms.usage: Does TM accept all strings? Undecidable by Rice's Theorem

visualTips.visualization: Draw a flowchart: Assume H(Program, Input) → returns Yes/No correctly → Build D using H that does opposite → Run D on itself → Paradox!
Use a Venn diagram: Outer circle = All languages, Middle = RE (semi-decidable), Inner = Recursive (decidable). Note: Some languages are not even RE.
Show arrows from known undecidable problem A → problem B. If we could decide B, we could decide A by transforming inputs.
Create a table: rows = TMs, columns = inputs. Diagonal = behavior on self. Construct new TM that differs at diagonal → Paradox!

analogies.analogy: Like asking 'Is this sentence true?' - creates infinite regression, no definitive yes/no answer exists.
Like predicting the future perfectly - you'd need to run the program to know, but then you'd be the program!
A library where you can find any book that's there (YES), but if a book isn't there, the librarian might never tell you - just keeps searching forever.
It's like asking 'Is this poem beautiful?' - you can't algorithmically determine non-trivial properties of what a TM computes.
If you can convert any 'solve A' question into a 'solve B' question, then solving B is at least as hard as solving A.
